text:
Kye Whyte made history by winningTeam GB’s first ever BMX medal at theOlympicsby scooping a silver at Tokyo 2020. After watching his team mate Bethany Shriever take gold in the women’s race moments later, the 24-year-old made headlines with a photograph showing him scoop up Shriever in his arms after her body cramped up at the finishing line.

The South-East Londoner has since gone on to win numerous other medals, including taking silver in the 2022 UCI BMX Racing World Championships in Nantes. And then in February of this year, Whyte won gold at the BMX Racing World Cup in Brisbane, beating Switzerland’s Cedric Butti by less than two hundredths of a second. He now has his sights set on continuing his winning streak at theParisgames. Below, we chatted to Whyte about what makes BMX such a stand-out sport and why, despite numerous injuries and setbacks, he always returns to it.

It was watching on the television the 2008summerOlympics inChina. That was when BMX made its Olympic debut and I remember being so excited to watch it. I was sat watching Liam Phillips compete as part of Team GB and thinking that is exactly what I want to do. I was around eight years old at the time and that was my goal, to get to the Olympics no matter what.

BMX racing is such a unique sport. I absolutely love the speeds we can go up too and the fact it is super-fast which is really exhilarating. It is one of the most dangerous sports and that’s what I love; the danger element pushes me on to go further. And when I win a race it is all worth it.

I don’t really find anything hard about it but you have to have the right mind set and concentration. Your goal is to race fast and always think about not falling off your bike at the same time. I always have to be focussed.

It is good to start at a young age if you like BMX riding, but there are people who develop a passion for it later in life. I’d really recommend getting involved in your local BMX club and there you can take part in training sessions and learn the basic skills. Then, if you are good enough, you can start competing in club races locally, then regional ones. There are local clubs all around the country who can teach you everything you need to know.

I got my first taste of racing at the Peckham BMX Club, where my dad was a coach. As a local club, it is excellent and helped teach me so much. I know my victory at the Olympics has inspired the next generation of BMX riders at the club too.

I knew from a very early age I was going to make it as I felt so confident on the bike. I was around ten year’s old and was winning races against boys who were four years older than me. I won lots of British junior titles and so I knew I was good at what I did. Knowing you are good at what you do powers you on to success.

It definitely has to be the numerous Injuries I have sustained from falling off the bike. I have had so many accidents and setbacks during my recovery. I have broken both my collarbones, shoulders, my wrist, fingers and the worst accident happened when I was 13 years old. I fell off the bike badly, broke my jaw and had a bleed on my brain. I was in an induced-coma for days and it was a very worrying time as I could have died. I couldn’t compete for a year after that accident. However, as with every setback I just recuperate and then get back on the bike. I love it too much.

I am so excited to get to Paris as I feel as though this is going to be my first Olympics experience as Tokyo had all the Covid restrictions in place. This time I’ll have my family watching on, my friends and the fans support means everything. You never know what’s going to happen as BMX racing is a hard one to judge but I’ll definitely have my eye on winning and bringing home a medal.

Kye Whyte is a P&G Ambassador for the Olympic Games Paris 2024

title: Team GB BMX legend Kye Whyte eyes up more Olympic glory | Metro News
description: Kye Whyte made history by winning Team GB's first ever BMX medal at the Tokyo Olympics 2020 and is eyeing more glory at Paris 2024.
